name: Dark-matter halo diversity problem
summary: Observed galactic rotation curves show enormous diversity in inner-region behaviour at fixed maximum circular velocity V_max - some galaxies have cuspy profiles, others nearly-flat cored profiles, at the same V_max. Cold-dark-matter N-body simulations (without baryons) predict tight scatter at fixed V_max. The 'diversity problem' (Oman et al. 2015, MNRAS 452, 3650, arXiv:1504.01437) cannot be resolved by stochastic baryonic feedback alone: simulations with feedback still produce tighter scatter than observed. Either DM physics is non-cold (self-interacting, fuzzy, warm) and the diversity reflects environmental history, or baryonic feedback is more strongly history-dependent than current models predict.

characteristic_numbers.note: Observed rotation-curve V(r=2 kpc) at V_max ~ 80 km/s varies over 30 km/s among similar-mass galaxies; pure-DM simulations predict much tighter ~5 km/s. From Oman et al. 2015 arXiv:1504.01437.

principles_stressed.note: Self-interacting DM (SIDM, sigma/m ~ 1 cm^2/g) naturally produces diverse cores; ultralight (fuzzy) DM produces core-cusp via wavefunction support. Either preferred over collisionless CDM if diversity is real, but tight LSS constraints discourage both at strong levels needed.

history: Cusp-core debate dates to 1990s (Moore 1994). Diversity problem articulated: Oman, Navarro, Fattahi, Frenk, Sawala, et al. (2015), 'The unexpected diversity of dwarf galaxy rotation curves', MNRAS 452, 3650, arXiv:1504.01437. Updated with SIDM-fit study: Kamada-Kaplinghat-Pace-Yu (2017, PRL 119, 111102, arXiv:1611.02716). Recent JWST + ALMA constraints add to the picture for high-z analogues.

references.citation: Oman et al., 'The unexpected diversity of dwarf galaxy rotation curves', MNRAS 452, 3650 (2015)
Kamada, Kaplinghat, Pace, Yu, 'Self-interacting dark matter can explain diverse galactic rotation curves', Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 111102 (2017)
